identity: N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC; acetylcysteine; LNAC; N-acetyl-3-mercapto-L-alanine). CAS 616-91-1. MW 163.20 g/mol. C5H9NO3S. Free thiol (sulfhydryl) form; usually supplied as the free acid (white-to-off-white crystalline hygroscopic powder, garlic-like odor). Salt forms used pharmaceutically: pH-adjusted with NaOH to sodium acetylcysteinate for injectable/inhalation solutions. Ionizable groups: carboxyl pKa ~3.2-3.3 and thiol pKa ~9.5 (sources: Cayman Chemical / ChemicalBook product monographs; Wikipedia Acetylcysteine summarizing pharmacopeia data). Glutathione (GSH) precursor / cysteine-delivery antioxidant.
r1_solubility: Aqueous solubility ~30 mg/mL (~30 g/L) in water at 25C per pharmaceutical-composition literature; freely soluble in water and ethanol, insoluble in ether/chloroform (Tier 2: ChemicalBook/Cayman monographs; US patent US20210251889A1 composition data). Compounded injectables exist up to 60 mg/mL and even 200-250 mg/mL with pH adjustment (Tier 2-3: PMC8463017 stability study; patent US20210251889A1), so the intrinsic solubility ceiling is NOT the limiting factor. In THIS formula NAC's defensible home is the DRY preload (no water), so in-solution solubility is moot there. IF anyone tried to put the capsule-equivalent dose (~50-90 mg/bird) into a small per-session liquid volume, even at the low ~30 mg/mL it would still dissolve in a few mL — so solubility headroom is adequate (>3x at any realistic drink concentration <=10 mg/mL). VERDICT R1: OK on pure solubility; the block on NAC-in-liquid is STABILITY/oxidation, not solubility.
r2_stability: Dominant degradation pathway in water = THIOL OXIDATION of the free -SH to the symmetric disulfide N,N'-diacetylcystine, dramatically accelerated by dissolved O2 and trace transition metals (Cu2+, Fe2/3+) and by alkaline pH; NAC is reported unstable above pH ~7.2 and most stable when kept mildly acidic (~pH 2-6) and oxygen/metal excluded (Tier 2-3: patent US20210251889A1 and US-series acetylcysteine composition patents state pH >7.2 instability, optimal pH 6.0-7.0 for buffered injectables; PMC8463017 documents acceptable stability of pH-adjusted 60 mg/mL injectable). Oxidative lability is shown directly by ~5% NAC recovery in 3% H2O2 at 60C vs 100% in plain water at 25C (Tier 3: patent composition data). Cu2+ catalyzes NAC->disulfide via thiyl-radical/Cu(I) intermediates (Tier 1: PMC9181168, ResearchGate DOI study "Reaction of N-Acetylcysteine with Cu2+"); Cu-catalyzed cysteine/NAC oxidation is a textbook model reaction (Tier 1: PMID 14990354). Photo/thermal sensitivity is secondary to the metal/O2 thiol oxidation. At the FORMULA pH ~5.8-6.2 NAC is within a tolerable window for short periods, BUT the liquids here contain citrate (metal-binding, partly protective) yet also Na-ascorbate/redox-active matrices and are stored wet with K-sorbate (only ~6% active at pH 6) — realistic wet shelf-life of an unprotected NAC drink is days-to-weeks with progressive thiol loss, not the 12+ months a dry powder gives. STORAGE: keep DRY, desiccated, foil/opaque, cool; dry shelf-life ~12-18 mo realistic. VERDICT R2: BLOCK for any wet/stored liquid SKU (thiol oxidation + ineffective preservative at pH 6); OK only as dry powder.
r3_conflicts: Pairwise conflicts at formula concentrations: (1) NAC + trace/added transition metals (Fe, Cu, and to a lesser extent the divalent Mg/Zn in Mg-glycerophosphate/Mg-malate/MgCl2 as impurity carriers) -> metal-catalyzed thiol oxidation to diacetylcystine, loss of antioxidant active and possible color change (mechanism Tier 1: PMC9181168, PMID 14990354). Mitigation: keep NAC dry and physically separate from any metal-containing premix; if ever co-formulated wet, add a chelator (EDTA/citrate) and exclude O2 — but dry preload sidesteps this entirely. (2) NAC + Na-ascorbate (S2): both are reducing agents; in a wet, metal-trace, near-neutral matrix they accelerate each other's oxidative turnover and can drive redox cycling -> mutual loss. Mitigation: never co-dissolve; ascorbate dry-and-separate, NAC dry preload. (3) NAC thiol can in principle react with reducing-sugar carbonyls, but the bigger Maillard risk in S2 is the amino acids/ribose, not NAC; still, keeping NAC out of the sugar matrix removes any thiol-carbonyl side reaction. (4) NAC + K-sorbate: sorbate is a poor preservative at pH 6 and does not protect NAC from oxidation; no direct adverse pairing, but it cannot rescue a wet NAC product. No precipitation/chelation issue with citrate/phosphate directly (NAC is not a polyvalent cation). NET: the only material conflicts are oxidative; all resolved by DRY separation.
avian_evidence: Avian (poultry) evidence is GOOD and directly supportive of a chronic dietary (dry-feed) role, NOT an acute drink role. (1) Heat-stress finisher broilers: dietary 2 g/kg NAC upregulated Nrf2 in ileum/tibia/bone marrow, raised antioxidant enzyme capacity, suppressed osteoclastogenesis and prevented heat-stress bone loss (Tier 1: J Anim Sci Biotechnol 2024, DOI 10.1186/s40104-024-01114-4, PMC11608502). (2) Chronic cyclic heat-stressed finisher broilers: NAC improved performance though without raising tissue glutathione (Tier 1: PMID 37782109, Br Poult Sci 64(6), DOI 10.1080/00071668.2023.2264234). (3) Cold-stressed broilers: 0.1% (1 g/kg) dietary NAC lowered plasma MDA and increased hepatic T-SOD/CAT/GPx under stress (Tier 1: PMC7468165, DOI 10.5713/ajas.19.0542). (4) Breeder hens + offspring: maternal dietary NAC improved antioxidant capacity, intestinal barrier, egg quality and offspring growth (Tier 1-2: Poult Sci 2025, ScienceDirect S0032579125009861). Effective DIETARY dose range ~1-2 g/kg feed (~0.1-0.2%), giving ~120-300 mg/bird/day at 120-150 g intake; design's ~0.3-0.5 g/kg feed (~50 mg/bird/day) is conservative and on the low end but within a reasonable antioxidant-support window. Mechanism = Nrf2/glutathione-axis antioxidant + intestinal-barrier + (relevant to gamefowl) pulmonary-HTN/ascites antioxidant context. ACUTE vs CHRONIC: all avian benefit is CHRONIC dietary loading over days-weeks; NO avian evidence supports a single acute in-fight sip of NAC. No documented avian toxicity at 1-2 g/kg feed.
verdict: ADD-strong as DRY PRELOAD (feed top-dress, ~7-10 d pre-event); SKIP/BLOCK in every wet liquid SKU. Recommended dose: ~0.3-0.5 g/kg feed (~50 mg/bird/day) for conservative antioxidant support, with headroom to 1-2 g/kg feed (the avian-validated range) if a stronger heat-stress/ascites antioxidant effect is wanted. Keep NAC OUT of the fight-day drink and out of S2-S5/F5: its free thiol oxidizes in water (metal/O2-catalyzed, worsens above pH ~6-7) and K-sorbate at pH 6 cannot preserve it -> R2 BLOCK for any stored wet product. Net: ADD-strong (preload, dry), BLOCK (any liquid). This matches the winning dry-first design which already relocates NAC from liquids to the dry preload.
cautions: Welfare/safety/producibility notes (non-blocking): (1) NAC benefit in birds is chronic/loading — a bird off-feed pre-event gets no NAC loading (timing/intake risk, not chemistry). (2) Hygroscopic + oxidation-prone: blend NAC LAST under low humidity, package with desiccant in opaque/foil sachets; moisture ingress regenerates the wet-oxidation problem even in "dry" product. (3) Garlic/sulfur odor and sour taste may reduce feed palatability at higher inclusion — monitor intake at >=1 g/kg feed. (4) Do NOT co-pack NAC with the metal premix (Mg/any Fe/Cu) or with ascorbate; keep micro-premixes physically separated to avoid slow solid-state/condensation oxidation. (5) Welfare-positive overall: antioxidant + intestinal-barrier + bone + pulmonary-HTN/ascites protection are bird-own-health benefits. (6) Pharmacological NAC megadoses (gram/kg BW) are not indicated here; feed-level 1-2 g/kg feed is the validated safe band — avoid extrapolating human bolus doses. (7) Effective avian dose (1-2 g/kg) is higher than the design's 0.3-0.5 g/kg; consider raising toward 1 g/kg if heat-stress is the target outcome.
sources: J Anim Sci Biotechnol 2024 'Improvement of antioxidant capability by dietary N-acetyl cysteine supplementation alleviates bone loss induced by chronic heat stress in finisher broilers' DOI 10.1186/s40104-024-01114-4 (PMC11608502) — Tier 1, 2 g/kg NAC, Nrf2 up, bone protection

Br Poult Sci 2023 64(6) 'N-acetyl-L-cysteine improves the performance of chronic cyclic heat-stressed finisher broilers but has no effect on tissue glutathione levels' PMID 37782109, DOI 10.1080/00071668.2023.2264234 — Tier 1, heat-stress performance

Asian-Australas J Anim Sci 2020 'Effects of N-acetylcysteine on the energy status and antioxidant capacity in heart and liver of cold-stressed broilers' DOI 10.5713/ajas.19.0542 (PMC7468165) — Tier 1, 0.1% dietary NAC, lowered MDA, raised hepatic SOD/CAT/GPx

Poult Sci 2025 'Dietary N-acetylcysteine supplementation improves antioxidant capacity and production performance of breeder hens and offspring' ScienceDirect S0032579125009861 — Tier 1-2, maternal NAC antioxidant/barrier
'Reaction of N-Acetylcysteine with Cu2+: Appearance of Intermediates with High Free Radical Scavenging Activity' PMC9181168 — Tier 1, Cu-catalyzed NAC thiol oxidation mechanism
'Inhibition of copper-catalyzed cysteine oxidation by nanomolar concentrations of iron salts' PMID 14990354 — Tier 1, metal-catalyzed thiol oxidation model
'Stability of N-Acetylcysteine 60 mg/mL in Extemporaneously Compounded Injectable Solutions' PMC8463017 — Tier 2, pH-adjusted aqueous stability
US Patent US20210251889A1 'N-Acetylcysteine Compositions and Methods' — Tier 3, states aqueous solubility ~30 mg/mL @25C, instability >pH 7.2, optimal pH 6.0-7.0, ~5% recovery in 3% H2O2 @60C
Cayman Chemical / ChemicalBook N-acetyl-L-cysteine monographs (CAS 616-91-1) — Tier 2, MW 163.2, pKa ~9.5 thiol, hygroscopic, water/ethanol soluble
Wikipedia 'Acetylcysteine' summarizing pharmacopeia data — Tier 2-3, identity/pKa cross-check only
blocking_gaps: Thiol pKa (~3.2 carboxyl / ~9.5 thiol) and the >pH 7.2 instability threshold rest partly on Tier 2-3 monograph/patent sources; a pharmacopeia (USP/Ph.Eur.) acetylcysteine monograph would upgrade confidence — UNKNOWN exact USP-stated aqueous stability pH window vs a primary citation.

No avian study tests an ACUTE single-dose NAC drink; the SKIP-in-liquid verdict for performance is by chemistry (oxidation) + absence of avian acute evidence, not a direct avian acute trial — treat 'no acute benefit' as evidence-gap, not disproven.

Exact aqueous solubility temperature curve and the metal/O2 oxidation rate at the precise formula pH 5.8-6.2 are inferred from patent/model-reaction data, not a dedicated NAC-in-this-matrix stability run — UNKNOWN quantitative wet shelf-life in the actual formula.

corrections: Blocking-gap overstatement: the dossier says 'no avian study tests an ACUTE single-dose NAC drink' and frames liquid NAC as having no avian evidence. A real avian NAC-in-DRINKING-WATER study exists and was beneficial: 'Effect of N-acetyl-L-cysteine administration in drinking water of broilers exposed to heat stress on performances, intestinal integrity, and oxidative status', Ital J Anim Sci 2024, DOI 10.1080/1828051X.2024.2339466 — NAC at 1000 mg/L in drinking water, Ross 308, improved intestinal integrity/antioxidant status. This is continuous chronic drink dosing (not a single in-fight sip), so the acute-sip gap stands and the chemistry BLOCK on STORED wet product still holds — but the dossier must correct the claim that NAC-in-liquid has no avian basis. Distinguish: (a) stored/shelf wet SKU = R2 BLOCK (thiol oxidation, sorbate ineffective at pH 6) vs (b) FRESH-mixed daily drink = avian-evidenced and chemically acceptable for same-day use. The dossier conflates these.

Solubility likely UNDERSTATED, not just conservative: the ~30 mg/mL @25C figure is patent-derived (Tier 3). Real NAC water solubility is far higher (pharmacopeial preparations reach ~100-200+ mg/mL; literature exists on WAX-COATING NAC specifically because it is too soluble: PMC9192162). Primary solubility data available: J Chem Eng Data 2023, DOI 10.1021/acs.jced.3c00458 (solid-liquid equilibrium in 12 solvents). R1 verdict (OK, not solubility-limited) is UNCHANGED and if anything more strongly OK; correct the 'low ~30 mg/mL' framing.

Upgrade pKa/identity citation from Tier 2-3 (Wikipedia/ChemicalBook/patent) to primary: 'Protonation Equilibria of N-Acetylcysteine', PMC7424727, gives carboxyl and thiol (~9.5) pKa from a peer-reviewed source. Closes one stated blocking gap.

PMID 14990354 framing is slightly misleading: its HEADLINE finding is that nanomolar iron salts INHIBIT copper-catalyzed cysteine oxidation (counterintuitive), not that iron worsens thiol oxidation. It does document Cu-catalyzed cysteine oxidation as baseline, so it is not fabricated, but cite it for the Cu mechanism only — do not imply Fe additively accelerates NAC oxidation here. A cleaner stability citation is available: 'Stability Study of Parenteral N-Acetylcysteine, and Chemical Inhibition of Its Dimerization', PMC9860916 (Tier 1-2), which directly documents aqueous NAC dimerization/oxidation kinetics and beats leaning on US patent composition data.
_verify.notes: Verified via WebSearch. All cited avian PMID/DOI are REAL and accurately summarized; doses correct and NOT inverted (2 g/kg bone/Nrf2 study PMC11608502; 0/500/1000/2000 mg/kg dose-response PMID 37782109 with the genuinely counterintuitive 'performance up but tissue GSH unchanged' detail correctly captured; 0.1% cold-stress PMC7468165; 1 g/kg breeder PMC9192162... breeder is S0032579125009861). Chemistry mechanism sources PMC9181168 and PMID 14990354 are real. No fabricated PMIDs found. Core verdict (ADD-strong dry preload / BLOCK stored wet SKU) is directionally correct and well-supported; downgraded from CONFIRMED to CORRECTED because (1) the 'no avian liquid evidence' blocking-gap is factually wrong given the drinking-water study, and (2) solubility framing and one mechanism citation need fixing. None of these reverse the verdict. Avian frame respected: feed-level 1-2 g/kg validated band, no human-bolus extrapolation, welfare-positive (antioxidant/intestinal-barrier/ascites-relevant).
